title: Ratios & Rates
blurb: Ratios compare amounts; rates compare different kinds of things. Master both and you can scale recipes, read maps, spot the best deal, and figure out speed — the math behind everyday choices.

lessons.title: What is a ratio?
lessons.concreteComparison: Imagine the perfect cup of hot chocolate: **2** spoons of cocoa for every **6** spoons of milk. That "2 to 6" is a ratio — it doesn't tell you how *much* you made, it tells you the **mix**. One spoonful or a giant pot, as long as you keep 2-to-6 it tastes the same.
lessons.objective: Understand what a ratio compares, and read and write a ratio three different ways.
concept: A **ratio** compares two amounts. We can write the cocoa-to-milk ratio three ways and they all mean the same thing: $2:6$, "2 to 6", or the fraction $\frac{2}{6}$.
**Order matters.** Cocoa-to-milk $2:6$ is not the same as milk-to-cocoa $6:2$ — one is a weak drink, the other is mud. Always match the numbers to the words in the order they're asked.

There are two flavours of ratio. A **part-to-part** ratio compares one group to another (3 apples to 5 oranges $= 3:5$). A **part-to-whole** ratio compares one group to the total (3 apples out of 8 fruit $= 3:8$). Read the question carefully to know which one it wants.
lessons.keyIdea: A ratio compares amounts by **division, not subtraction** — it answers "how many of one for each of the other?", and the order of the numbers must match the words.

tryThis.title: Ratio hunt in your kitchen
tryThis.body: Open the cutlery drawer and count the forks and the spoons. Write the ratio of forks to spoons, then the ratio of forks to the total number of utensils. Two different ratios from the same drawer!

facts.title: Three ways to write a ratio
facts.explanation: All three notations mean the same comparison. The fraction form is handy because it lets you use everything you already know about fractions.

facts.title: Part-to-whole
facts.explanation: The total is the sum of all the parts. If there are 3 red and 5 blue, the total is 8.

workedExamples.statement: A fruit bowl has **4 apples** and **6 oranges**. Write the ratio of apples to oranges in simplest form.
workedExamples.note: "Apples to oranges" means apples first. Both numbers share a common factor, so we can simplify — just like a fraction.
workedExamples.solution: Apples : oranges $= 4 : 6$. Both 4 and 6 divide by 2, so $4:6 = 2:3$. **Answer: $2:3$** (for every 2 apples there are 3 oranges).

workedExamples.statement: A class has **10 girls** and **15 boys**. What is the ratio of girls to the **total** number of students?
workedExamples.note: "Total" is the signal for a part-to-whole ratio. First find the total, then compare.
workedExamples.solution: Total students $= 10 + 15 = 25$. Girls : total $= 10 : 25$. Both divide by 5: $10:25 = 2:5$. **Answer: $2:5$** (2 of every 5 students are girls).

workedExamples.statement: In a bag of marbles there are **3 red** and **5 blue**. Give (a) red-to-blue and (b) red-to-total.
workedExamples.note: Same marbles, two different questions — watch which words you're matching.
workedExamples.solution: (a) Red : blue $= 3:5$ (already simplest). (b) Total $= 3+5 = 8$, so red : total $= 3:8$. **Answers: $3:5$ and $3:8$.**

commonMistakes.text: Writing the numbers in the wrong order — answering $3:5$ when the question asks for the ratio of the *second* group to the first.
commonMistakes.correction: Match each number to the word right next to it, left to right.

commonMistakes.text: Mixing up part-to-part and part-to-whole — giving $3:5$ when the question asks "out of the total".
commonMistakes.correction: The word "total" or "out of all" means add the parts first.

commonMistakes.text: Subtracting the amounts (6 − 2 = 4) instead of comparing them.
commonMistakes.correction: A ratio compares by division, not subtraction.

tryIt.statement: A team has 7 wins and 4 losses. Ratio of wins to **total** games?

steps.title: What is a ratio?
beats: A **ratio** compares two amounts — it tells you the **mix**, not how much you made.
This hot chocolate recipe is **2 scoops of cocoa** for every **6 cups of milk**.
We write that as $2:6$ (read: "2 to 6"). One cup or a giant pot — keep 2-to-6 and it tastes the same.
A ratio is about the **mix**, not the total amount.

steps.title: Scale the recipe
steps.teach: Slide to make more batches — both amounts grow, but the mix never changes. **The ratio is the mix.**

steps.title: Why the recipe still tastes the same
beats: Every new batch **multiplies both numbers by the same amount**: 2 batches → $4:12$, 3 batches → $6:18$.
Because you scaled both parts equally, the mix — the ratio — stays the same.
This connects to what you'll learn next: $2:6$, $4:12$, and $6:18$ are all **equivalent ratios**.
The ratio $2:6$ is not a recipe for "this much" hot chocolate — it's the recipe for **any amount**.

steps.title: Reading a ratio
steps.intro: Read the two amounts, then write them in the order asked.

examples.prompt: 5 apples and 3 oranges — write the ratio of **apples to oranges**.
steps: Apples are named first, so apples go first.

examples.prompt: 2 red and 7 blue marbles — write **red to blue**.
steps: Red is named first: red goes first.

steps.title: Your turn
steps.intro: Write each ratio in the order asked. The picture shows who goes first.

problems.prompt: 4 cats and 9 dogs — ratio of **cats to dogs**?
problems.explanation: Cats are named first, so cats go first: $4:9$.

problems.prompt: 3 stars and 8 moons — ratio of **stars to moons**?

steps.title: Did the mix hold?
steps.prompt: 1 batch is **2 cocoa : 6 milk**. What is **3 batches**?
steps.explanation: Multiply both by 3 → $6:18$. The mix is still the same.

steps.title: Three ways to write the same ratio
beats: The ratio $2:6$ can be written three ways — and they all mean exactly the same comparison.
**Colon notation:** $2:6$ — the most common way you'll see ratios.
**Word form:** "2 to 6" — used in everyday language.
**Fraction form:** $\dfrac{2}{6}$ — handy because you can use everything you already know about fractions.

steps.title: Tap each notation
steps.teach: Three costumes, one ratio. Tap each one and see it switch — the comparison never changes.

examples.prompt: 5 dogs and 8 cats — write **dogs to cats** three ways.
steps: All three are the same comparison.

examples.prompt: 3 wins and 4 losses — write **wins to losses** three ways.
steps: $3:4$, "3 to 4", and $\dfrac{3}{4}$ — all three mean the same thing.

problems.prompt: Write **2 to 9** as a ratio $a:b$.
Write the fraction $\dfrac{4}{7}$ as a ratio.
problems.explanation: $\dfrac{4}{7}$ and $4:7$ are the same comparison — same order, same numbers.

steps.title: Which one is different?
steps.prompt: Which is **NOT** another way to write $3:5$?
steps.explanation: $5:3$ flips the order — that is a different ratio. $\dfrac{3}{5}$ and "3 to 5" are both the same as $3:5$.

steps.title: Order matters — a lot
beats: Look at the picture: **2 cocoa scoops, 6 cups of milk** — that is a smooth, drinkable hot chocolate.
Now flip it: **6 cocoa scoops, 2 cups of milk** — same numbers, very different drink. That is basically mud.
So $2:6$ and $6:2$ are **not** the same ratio. Order always matches the words.
Rule: the first number belongs to whichever word comes first. Match them left to right, every time.

steps.title: Flip the order
steps.teach: Tap to flip the ratio. Notice $2:6$ is a smooth drink — $6:2$ is mud. Same numbers, totally different mix.

steps.title: Reversing a ratio
steps.intro: Reversing the order swaps the two numbers — and makes a completely different ratio.

examples.prompt: A snack is **1 raisin : 3 nuts**. What is the ratio of **nuts to raisins**?
steps: The question asks nuts first — so swap the numbers.

examples.prompt: Wins to losses is **5:2**. Write **losses to wins**.
steps: Losses are now named first — put losses first.

problems.prompt: Dogs to cats is **4:7**. What is **cats to dogs**?
problems.explanation: Cats go first now — reverse the order: $7:4$.

problems.prompt: A paint mix is **2 blue : 5 white**. Is **5 blue : 2 white** the same mix?
options: No — it is a different ratio
problems.explanation: $2:5$ has much more white; $5:2$ has much more blue. Same numbers, very different paint colours.

steps.title: Which order gives a smooth drink?
steps.prompt: You want **smooth** hot chocolate (2 cocoa, 6 milk). Which ratio is it?
steps.explanation: $2:6$ is 2 cocoa to 6 milk — smooth. $6:2$ would be 6 cocoa to 2 milk — mud.

steps.title: Two kinds of ratio
beats: Look at the picture: 3 apples and 5 oranges sitting side by side — that is **8 pieces of fruit** total.
**Part-to-part** compares the two groups against each other: apples to oranges $= 3:5$.
**Part-to-whole** compares one group to the full total: apples to all fruit $= 3:8$ (because $3+5=8$).
The word **"total"** or **"out of all"** is your signal to add the parts first, then compare.

steps.title: Compare the two
steps.teach: **Part-to-part** compares the two groups ($3:5$). **Part-to-whole** compares one group to the total ($3:8$).

examples.prompt: A bag has **3 red** and **5 blue** marbles. Write (a) red to blue, and (b) red to the total.
A class has **4 boys** and **6 girls**. Write **boys to the total**.

steps.title: Your turn
steps.intro: Separated groups = part-to-part. One group circled inside the total = part-to-whole.

problems.prompt: **2 cats, 6 dogs** — cats to dogs (just the two groups)?
**2 cats, 6 dogs** — cats to **all the animals**?
problems.explanation: Total $= 2+6 = 8$, so cats to all animals: $2:8$.

problems.prompt: **5 apples, 3 bananas** — apples to the total?

steps.title: Part-to-part or part-to-whole?
steps.prompt: **3 apples, 5 oranges.** What is apples to the **total**?
steps.explanation: Total $= 3+5 = 8$. Apples to total: $3:8$. (The key word was "total" — add first!)

steps.title: What you learned
points: A **ratio** compares two amounts — it gives the **mix**, not the total.
Write it three ways: $a:b$, "$a$ to $b$", or $\dfrac{a}{b}$.
**Order matters**: $2:6$ (smooth drink) and $6:2$ (mud) are different ratios.
**Part-to-part** compares two groups ($3:5$); **part-to-whole** compares one group to the total ($3:8$) — add the parts first.

steps.title: Time to practice
steps.body: You have learned all four ideas — reading a ratio, writing it three ways, order, and part-to-part vs part-to-whole. Lock them in with this mixed set. Tap a wrong choice and just try again.

steps.title: Mixed practice
steps.intro: A little of everything you learned.

problems.prompt: **8 red and 3 green** counters — ratio of **red to green**?
problems.explanation: Red is named first, so red goes first: $8:3$.

problems.prompt: Which is another way to write $5:2$?
Cats to dogs is **3:7**. What is **dogs to cats**?
problems.explanation: Dogs go first now — reverse the order: $7:3$.

problems.prompt: **4 apples and 6 oranges** — apples to the **total**?
**2 blue and 5 red** — blue to red (just the two groups)?
**3 cats and 5 dogs** — cats to **all the animals**?

steps.title: You have finished "What is a ratio?"
steps.body: You can now read a ratio, write it three ways, respect its order, and tell part-to-part from part-to-whole. Next up: **equivalent ratios** — making bigger and smaller batches with the same mix.

lessons.title: Equivalent ratios
lessons.concreteComparison: You make pancakes with **1** egg for every **2** cups of flour. Tomorrow your whole family visits, so you use **3** eggs and **6** cups. Different amounts, *same pancakes* — because $1:2$ and $3:6$ are **equivalent ratios**. Scaling a recipe up or down is exactly this idea.
lessons.objective: Build equivalent ratios by multiplying or dividing both parts, and reduce a ratio to simplest form.
concept: Two ratios are **equivalent** if you can get one from the other by multiplying (or dividing) **both** numbers by the same amount. $1:2$, $2:4$, $3:6$, $10:20$ are all the same ratio.
A **ratio table** lines up equivalent ratios so you can spot the missing value. Each new column scales the one before it by the same factor.
The **simplest form** of a ratio is found by dividing both numbers by their greatest common factor (GCF). $18:24$ both divide by 6, giving $3:4$ — you can't shrink it further.
lessons.keyIdea: Multiply or divide **both** numbers by the **same** amount and the ratio stays equivalent. Add a number to both, and it changes — so never add.

tryThis.title: Double, then triple
tryThis.body: Pick a snack you like that mixes two things (say 2 scoops of raisins to 3 scoops of nuts). Build a ratio table: write the mix for 1 batch, 2 batches, and 3 batches. Notice both numbers grow by the same factor.

facts.title: Scaling a ratio
facts.explanation: Multiply both parts by any non-zero number $k$ to get an equivalent ratio.

facts.title: Simplest form
facts.explanation: Divide both parts by their greatest common factor to reduce the ratio.

workedExamples.note: Find the biggest number that divides both. The GCF of 18 and 24 is 6.

workedExamples.statement: Fill the ratio table for $2:5$ — find the partners for 4 and for 6 on the left.
workedExamples.note: Whatever you multiply the left number by, multiply the right by the same.
workedExamples.solution: $2:5$. Double it: $4:10$. Triple it: $6:15$. So the table reads $2:5,\;4:10,\;6:15$.

workedExamples.note: How do you turn 3 into 9? Multiply by 3. Do the same to the 4.
workedExamples.solution: $3 \times 3 = 9$, so multiply the right side by 3 too: $4 \times 3 = 12$. **Answer: $9:12$.**

commonMistakes.text: Adding the same number to both parts ($2:3 \to 3:4$ by adding 1) and thinking it's equivalent.
commonMistakes.correction: Equivalent ratios come from multiplying/dividing both parts, never adding.
Whatever you do to one number, do to the other.

steps.title: Same pancakes, bigger batch
beats: Your pancake recipe: **1 egg** for every **2 cups of flour** — that's the ratio $1:2$.
For the whole family you use **3 eggs** and **6 cups of flour**. Different amounts, but the pancakes taste exactly the same.
Why? You multiplied **both** numbers by 3. Multiply both by the same number and the mix doesn't change — you get an **equivalent ratio**.
Remember from **What Is a Ratio?**: a ratio is the mix, not the amount. Equivalent ratios are just *bigger or smaller batches of the same mix*.

steps.title: Build the ratio table
steps.teach: Tap the columns to add batches. Both numbers grow together — the mix never changes. That's **equivalent ratios**.

steps.title: Why the table works
beats: Each new column multiplies **both** numbers by the same factor — so every column is still the ratio $1:2$.
To **find a missing value**, ask: what did I multiply one side by? Then do the same to the other.
Example: $1:2 = 5:\;?$ → the 1 became 5 ($\times 5$), so multiply the 2 by 5 too → $10$.
Never **add** the same number to both parts. Adding gives a different ratio — only multiplying keeps the mix.

steps.title: Finding the missing number
steps.intro: Whatever you multiply one part by, multiply the other by the same.

examples.prompt: Find the missing number: $2 : 3 = 8 : \;?$
steps: Do the same to the second: $3 \times 4 = 12$.

examples.prompt: Find the missing number: $5 : 2 = \;? : 10$
steps: Do the same to the first: $5 \times 5 = 25$.

steps.title: Your turn — scale both parts
steps.intro: Spot the multiplier on one side, then apply it to the other.

problems.prompt: Ratio table: $2 : 7 = 4 : \;?$
Is $4:6$ equivalent to $10:15$?

steps.title: Mongolian süütei tsai in a kettle
steps.body: Süütei tsai (Mongolian milk tea) uses about $2:3$ — 2 parts milk to 3 parts water. A tiny cup or a giant pot, as long as you keep $2:3$ (or $4:6$, or $10:15$) it tastes exactly right. Equivalent ratios in every sip.

steps.title: Shrinking a ratio to its simplest form
beats: You can also go the other way — **shrink** a ratio by dividing both parts by the same number.
The key is the **greatest common factor (GCF)**: the biggest number that divides evenly into both parts.
Example: $4:6$ — the GCF is $2$. Divide both by 2: $4\div2=2$ and $6\div2=3$ → **simplest form $2:3$**.
A ratio is in simplest form when the only number that divides evenly into both parts is $1$.

steps.title: The simplest column
steps.teach: Every column here is equivalent — but the **first** one, $2:3$, uses the smallest whole numbers possible. That's **simplest form**.

steps.title: Reducing to simplest form
steps.intro: Find the GCF, then divide both parts by it.

examples.prompt: Write $18 : 24$ in simplest form.
steps: List factors: GCF of 18 and 24 is $6$.

examples.prompt: Write $20 : 30$ in simplest form.

steps.title: Your turn — divide both by the GCF
steps.intro: Divide both parts by their greatest common factor.

problems.prompt: Write $18:24$ in simplest form.
Write $20:30$ in simplest form.
Which is **NOT** equivalent to $3:8$?
problems.explanation: $6:16$, $15:40$, and $12:32$ all reduce to $3:8$. $9:20$ does not — it reduces to $9:20$.

steps.title: What you learned
points: **Multiply or divide both** parts by the same number → you get an equivalent ratio. The mix stays the same.
**Never add** the same number to both — that changes the ratio.
A **ratio table** lines up equivalents so you can read off or fill in any missing value.
**Simplest form**: divide both parts by their greatest common factor (GCF).

steps.title: Time to practice
steps.body: A mix of both ideas — find missing values, build equivalents, and reduce to simplest form. Tap a wrong choice and just try again.

steps.title: Mixed practice
steps.intro: A little of everything you learned.

problems.prompt: Write $15 : 25$ in simplest form.
Is $6:10$ equivalent to $9:15$?
Write $14 : 21$ in simplest form.
A ratio table reads $3:4$, $6:8$, $9:\;?$. What is the missing value?
problems.explanation: Each step multiplies both by the next whole number. $4 \times 3 = 12$.

steps.title: You've finished "Equivalent Ratios"
steps.body: You can scale a ratio up, find a missing value with a ratio table, and shrink any ratio to its simplest form. Maps, recipes, and mixing paint all run on this. Next up: **comparing ratios** — which mix is stronger?

lessons.title: Comparing ratios
lessons.concreteComparison: Two jugs of orange squash: one mixes **2** cups juice to **3** cups water, the other **3** to **5**. Which tastes more orange? You can't just eyeball it — copy each mix until the waters match, then compare the juice. It's the same skill that tells you which paint mix is darker or which class has more girls per boy.
lessons.objective: Decide which of two mixes tastes stronger by copying each until the waters match, or by cross-multiplying.
concept: Copy each mix until both have the same water, then compare the juice — more juice tastes more orange. For $2:3$ vs $3:5$, copy to 15 water each: 10 juice vs 9, so $2:3$ tastes more orange.
The quick shortcut is **cross-multiplication**: to compare $\frac{a}{b}$ and $\frac{c}{d}$, compare $a\times d$ with $c\times b$. The side with the bigger product tastes stronger.
If the two products are equal, the mixes **taste exactly the same** — equivalent ratios (same taste, different size).
lessons.keyIdea: To compare two mixes, copy each until the waters match, then compare the juice — more juice tastes more orange. Cross-multiplying is the quick shortcut.

facts.title: Cross-multiply to compare
facts.explanation: Bigger cross-product means bigger ratio. Equal products mean equivalent ratios.

workedExamples.statement: Which has more orange taste, **3 : 4** or **5 : 6**?
workedExamples.note: Copy until the waters match, or cross-multiply.
workedExamples.solution: Cross-multiply: $3\times 6 = 18$ and $5\times 4 = 20$. Since $20 > 18$, **$5:6$ has more orange taste** (as values, $\frac{5}{6}\approx 0.83 > \frac{3}{4}=0.75$).

workedExamples.statement: Which has more orange taste, **2 : 3** or **4 : 5**?
workedExamples.solution: $2\times 5 = 10$ and $4\times 3 = 12$. Since $12 > 10$, **$4:5$ has more orange taste** ($\frac{4}{5}=0.8 > \frac{2}{3}\approx 0.67$).

commonMistakes.text: Comparing only the first numbers ("5 beats 3, so $5:6$ tastes stronger") — which sometimes gives the right answer by luck and sometimes doesn't.
commonMistakes.correction: Always compare the whole mix: copy until the waters match, or cross-multiply.

commonMistakes.text: Deciding by the size of the gap ($3:4$ has a gap of 1, $5:6$ has a gap of 1, so "equal").
commonMistakes.correction: The difference between the parts doesn't measure a ratio — its value does.

tryIt.statement: Which has more orange taste, **5 : 8** or **7 : 10**?
tryIt.solution: Cross-multiply: $5\times10=50$, $7\times8=56$. $56>50$, so $7:10$ has more orange taste.

tryIt.statement: Which has more orange taste, **7 : 9** or **8 : 11**?
tryIt.solution: Cross-multiply: $7\times11=77$, $8\times9=72$. $77>72$, so $7:9$ has more orange taste.

steps.title: Which jug has more orange taste?
steps.teach: Two jugs of orange squash — different mixes. Which one *tastes* more orange? Tap **Make the water equal**, then the taller orange bar wins.

steps.title: Why making the water equal works
beats: Remember from **Equivalent Ratios**: if you multiply **both** numbers by the same number, the mix tastes exactly the same — it's just a bigger batch.
**Make the water equal** uses that idea — it copies each mix (Jug A ×5, Jug B ×3) until both jugs hold the same water, $3 \times 5 = 15$.
Now the waters match, so the only thing left to compare is the **juice**: $10$ vs $9$ → Jug A tastes more orange.
The quick shortcut for this is **cross-multiplying**: $2 \times 5 = 10$ vs $3 \times 3 = 9$ — multiply across the corners.

steps.title: Try it with 3 : 4 and 5 : 6
steps.teach: Same move every time: make the water equal, then the taller orange bar wins. Which jug has more orange taste?

steps.title: Which has more orange taste?
steps.intro: Picture copying each mix until the waters match — or cross-multiply. Tap your answer.

problems.prompt: Which has more orange taste — **3 : 5** or **4 : 7**?
problems.explanation: Cross-multiply: $3 \times 7 = 21$ vs $4 \times 5 = 20$. $21 > 20$, so $3:5$ has more orange taste.

problems.prompt: Which paint is bluer — **5 : 8** blue:white or **7 : 10** blue:white?

steps.title: Which tastes more orange?
steps.prompt: Jug A is **2 : 3**, Jug B is **3 : 5**. Which jug has more orange taste?
steps.explanation: Copy by the other jug's water (×5 and ×3): the juice becomes $10$ vs $9$. Jug A has more orange taste.

steps.title: Traders did this for a living
steps.body: Merchants on the Silk Road — which crossed Mongolia — sized up deals like **3 silks for 5 coins** against **2 silks for 3 coins** all day long. Same idea you just used: make one side match, then compare.

steps.title: When two jugs taste exactly the same
steps.teach: These two jugs are different sizes, but they can **taste exactly the same** — that's *equivalent ratios*. If you copied Jug A enough, you'd get exactly Jug B. Tap **Make the water equal** to copy each one and watch them become the same mix.

steps.title: Do 1 : 2 and 2 : 4 taste the same?
steps.teach: Different numbers can still be the very same mix. Copy each one until the waters match and see if they taste the same.

steps.title: Do they taste the same?
steps.intro: Cross-multiply: if both numbers match, the mixes taste exactly the same.

problems.prompt: Do **2 : 5** and **4 : 10** taste the same?
Do **3 : 5** and **6 : 8** taste the same?
One paint is **3 blue : 7 white**, another is **6 : 14**. Same colour?

steps.title: Same taste?
steps.explanation: Cross-multiply: $2 \times 12 = 24$ and $3 \times 8 = 24$. Both $24$ → same taste (both are $2:3$).

steps.title: What you learned
points: To compare two mixes, **copy each one until the waters match**, then compare the juice.
The shortcut is **cross-multiplying**: for $a:b$ vs $c:d$, compare $a \times d$ with $b \times c$.
**More juice (the bigger cross-number) = more orange taste.** Never just compare the first numbers.
If the two cross-numbers are **equal**, the mixes **taste exactly the same** — equivalent ratios.

steps.title: Time to practice
steps.body: You've got both moves — finding which jug tastes stronger, and spotting jugs that taste the same. Run this mixed set to lock them in. Tap a wrong choice and just try again.

steps.title: Mixed practice
steps.intro: A little of everything you learned.

problems.prompt: Which has more orange taste — **1 : 2** or **3 : 7**?
Do **5 : 8** and **10 : 16** taste the same?
Which has more orange taste — **4 : 5** or **5 : 6**?
Jug A is **3 : 4**, Jug B is **5 : 7**. Which jug has more orange taste?
Do **6 : 9** and **4 : 7** taste the same?
Which has more orange taste — **7 : 9** or **8 : 11**?

steps.title: You've finished "Comparing Ratios"
steps.body: You can now compare any two mixes by copying until the waters match — or cross-multiplying, the same trick — and spot jugs that taste the same in a flash. Next up: **rates**, when the two things you compare have *different units*, like kilometres and hours.

lessons.title: Rates
lessons.concreteComparison: When a car sign says **120 km in 2 hours**, that's a **rate** — it compares two *different* kinds of things: distance and time. Your heartbeat (beats per minute), your typing (words per minute), a phone's download (MB per second) are all rates hiding in plain sight.
lessons.objective: Recognise a rate as a ratio of two different units and calculate it.
concept: A **rate** is a ratio that compares two **different** units. "120 km : 2 hours" compares kilometres to hours — unlike a ratio such as apples-to-oranges where both are 'pieces of fruit'.
Because the units are different, a rate **always carries its units** when you write it: 60 km/h, 15 pages/min, 9 m/s. Drop the units and the number means nothing.
You compute a rate the same way you simplify a ratio — by dividing — but you keep track of what each number measures.
lessons.keyIdea: A rate compares two different kinds of things (like distance and time). Always write the units — "15" is meaningless, "15 pages per minute" is a rate.

tryThis.title: Find your writing rate
tryThis.body: Set a 30-second timer and write your full name over and over until it stops. Count how many times you wrote it, then double it — that's your name-writing rate in **names per minute**.

facts.title: What a rate is
facts.latex: $\text{rate} = \dfrac{\text{amount of one thing}}{\text{amount of a different thing}}$
facts.explanation: The two quantities have different units, so the answer is 'something per something'.

workedExamples.statement: A printer prints **90 pages in 6 minutes**. What is its rate in pages per minute?
workedExamples.note: Pages and minutes are different units — this is a rate. Divide pages by minutes.
workedExamples.solution: $\frac{90 \text{ pages}}{6 \text{ minutes}} = 15$ pages/minute. **Answer: 15 pages per minute.**

workedExamples.statement: A car travels **150 miles in 3 hours**. Find its speed.
workedExamples.note: Speed is a rate: distance per time.

commonMistakes.text: Writing just the number ("the rate is 15") and forgetting the units.
commonMistakes.correction: A rate needs its units: 15 pages per minute.
Match the order to the words: 'pages per minute' means pages ÷ minutes.

tryIt.statement: A runner covers **240 km in 4 hours**. Find the speed.
You type **60 words in 2 minutes**. What is your rate?
A tap fills **84 litres in 7 minutes**. Find the rate in litres per minute.

steps.title: A ratio of two DIFFERENT things
beats: You already know a ratio compares two amounts — like 2 cocoa scoops to 6 milk cups. Both are measured in *scoops* or *cups*, the same kind of thing.
A **rate** is a ratio where the two things are **completely different kinds** — like kilometres and hours, pages and minutes, or beats and seconds.
Because the units are different, you **must keep the units** when you write a rate. "60" means nothing. "60 km/h" is a rate — it tells you exactly what was measured.
To find a rate, **divide** the first amount by the second: $120 \text{ km} \div 2 \text{ h} = 60$ km/h — how far you go in **one** hour.

steps.title: Build a speed
steps.teach: Change the distance or the time — the rate updates instantly. More distance in the same time = faster. Same distance in less time = faster too. The rate is always **distance ÷ time**.

steps.title: Why we divide — and why units stay
beats: A rate is just the **equivalent ratio with 1 on the bottom**: scale $120:2$ down until the bottom is $1$ and you get $60:1$ — that is **60 km per 1 hour**.
$\dfrac{120 \text{ km}}{2 \text{ h}}$ means: for every 1 hour, how many km? Divide both sides by 2 → $\dfrac{60 \text{ km}}{1 \text{ h}}$, which we write **60 km/h**.
The word **per** always means ÷ (divide). "Pages **per** minute" = pages ÷ minutes. "Beats **per** second" = beats ÷ seconds.
Drop the units and the number is useless — "60" could be anything. Keep them: **60 km/h** is a speed; **60 pages/min** is a reading pace.

steps.title: Computing a speed
steps.intro: Spot the two different units, then divide the top by the bottom and keep the units.

examples.prompt: A car travels **90 km in 3 hours**. How fast is it going?
steps: Speed is a rate: distance ÷ time.

examples.prompt: A cyclist covers **60 km in 4 hours**. What is the speed?

steps.title: Your turn — find the speed
steps.intro: Divide the distance by the time. Pick the correct answer.

problems.prompt: A bus travels **120 km in 2 hours**. What is its speed?
A runner covers **45 km in 3 hours**. What is the speed?

steps.title: Spot the rate
steps.prompt: A car goes **180 km in 3 h**. What is the speed?
steps.explanation: Divide: $180 \div 3 = 60$ km/h. Keep the units — km/h, not just 60!

steps.title: Rates hide in every sport
steps.body: The 100 m world record sprint takes about **9.58 seconds** — a rate of roughly **10.44 metres per second**. That's faster than most cyclists on flat ground. Rates let us compare things that would otherwise be impossible to line up side by side.

steps.title: Rates are everywhere — not just speed
beats: Speed (km/h) is the most famous rate, but the same **divide and keep the units** move works for any two different quantities.
A printer making **84 pages in 7 minutes** → $84 \div 7 = 12$ **pages per minute**.
A tap filling **90 litres in 6 minutes** → $90 \div 6 = 15$ **litres per minute**.
A healthy heart beating **280 times in 4 minutes** → $280 \div 4 = 70$ **beats per minute**. Same idea every time: divide the top by the bottom, keep both unit names.

steps.title: Items packed per minute
steps.teach: A factory line packs items into boxes. Change the number of items or the minutes and the rate updates. The unit is **items/min** — a different pair of units from km/h, but the exact same move: top ÷ bottom.

steps.title: All kinds of rates
steps.intro: "Per" always means divide. Divide the first quantity by the second and keep both unit names.

examples.prompt: A printer prints **84 pages in 7 minutes**. What is the rate in pages per minute?
A tap fills **90 litres in 6 minutes**. What is the flow rate?

steps.title: Your turn — rates beyond speed
steps.intro: Divide the first quantity by the second and choose the correct rate.

problems.prompt: You read **72 pages in 6 minutes**. What is your reading rate?
A hose fills **75 litres in 5 minutes**. What is the flow rate?
A heart beats **280 times in 4 minutes**. What is the rate in beats per minute?

steps.title: Factory line rate
steps.prompt: A factory packs **96 items in 8 minutes**. What is the packing rate?
steps.explanation: $96 \div 8 = 12$ items per minute. Divide, then keep the units.

steps.title: What you learned
points: A **rate** is a ratio of two **different** units — km and hours, pages and minutes, litres and minutes.
Compute a rate by **dividing** the first quantity by the second: $\text{rate} = \dfrac{\text{top quantity}}{\text{bottom quantity}}$.
**Always keep the units** — "15" is meaningless; "15 pages per minute" is a rate.
"**Per**" means divide: pages **per** minute $=$ pages $\div$ minutes.

steps.title: Time to practice
steps.body: You've seen rates in distance/time, pages/time, litres/time, and beats/time. The move is always the same: **divide and keep the units**. Work through this mixed set to lock it in.

steps.title: Mixed practice
steps.intro: Different units, same idea: divide the first by the second and keep the unit names.

problems.prompt: A car travels **180 km in 3 hours**. What is the speed?
A cyclist covers **90 km in 6 hours**. What is the speed?
A printer prints **60 pages in 4 minutes**. What is the rate?
A tap fills **84 litres in 7 minutes**. What is the flow rate?
Which of these is **not** a rate?
problems.explanation: Apples and oranges are the **same kind of thing** (fruit) — that's a ratio, not a rate. A rate must compare two **different** units.

problems.prompt: A heart beats **420 times in 6 minutes**. What is the rate?

steps.title: You've finished "Rates"
steps.body: You can now spot a rate (two different units), compute it (divide!), and keep the units attached. Rates hide in every number with a **per** in it — on speedometers, food labels, download bars, and even your own heartbeat. Next up: **unit rates** — always scaling a rate down to a single unit to make comparisons easy.

lessons.title: Unit rate
lessons.concreteComparison: At the shop, the same juice comes in two sizes: **$6 for 3 bottles** or **$10 for 4 bottles**. Which is the better deal? You can't tell until you find the price for **one** bottle — the **unit rate**. Unit rates are the secret weapon of every smart shopper.
lessons.objective: Find a unit rate (the amount for exactly one) and use unit rates to choose the better buy.
concept: A **unit rate** is a rate with a denominator of **1** — the amount for exactly one unit. You get it by dividing the top number by the bottom number.
Unit rates make comparisons fair. "\$6 for 3" and "\$10 for 4" are hard to compare directly, but "\$2 each" versus "\$2.50 each" is obvious.
The cheaper unit price is the better buy; a higher unit rate (like km per litre) is usually the more efficient choice — read the question to know whether bigger or smaller is 'better'.
lessons.keyIdea: A unit rate is the amount for exactly **one** — divide the top by the bottom. To find the best deal, compare unit prices, not totals.

tryThis.title: Be the best-buy detective
tryThis.body: Find two sizes of the same product in your kitchen (or a shop flyer). Divide each price by the number (or grams) to get the price per unit. Which size is actually cheaper per unit? Sometimes the big one isn't!

facts.title: Unit rate
facts.explanation: Dividing gives the amount per single unit — per item, per kilogram, per hour.

workedExamples.statement: **$12 for 4 notebooks.** What is the price per notebook?
workedExamples.note: Per one notebook means divide the price by the number of notebooks.

workedExamples.statement: A car drives **300 km on 25 litres** of fuel. Find the unit rate in km per litre.
workedExamples.note: Per one litre — divide distance by litres.
workedExamples.solution: $\frac{300 \text{ km}}{25 \text{ L}} = 12$ km per litre. **Answer: 12 km/L.**

workedExamples.statement: Which is the better deal: **$6 for 3 bottles** or **$10 for 4 bottles**?
workedExamples.note: Find each unit price, then compare — cheaper per bottle wins.
workedExamples.solution: First: $\frac{\$6}{3} = \$2$ per bottle. Second: $\frac{\$10}{4} = \$2.50$ per bottle. Since $\$2 < \$2.50$, **the $6-for-3 pack is the better deal.**

commonMistakes.text: Picking the cheaper total price as 'the deal' without checking the size.
commonMistakes.correction: Compare price per unit, not the total — the dearer pack can be cheaper per item.

commonMistakes.text: Dividing the wrong way (bottles ÷ dollars), getting 'bottles per dollar' when you wanted price per bottle.
commonMistakes.correction: Match the words: 'price per bottle' = price ÷ bottles.

tryIt.statement: A bike covers **180 miles on 6 gallons**. Unit rate in miles per gallon?
Better deal: **$9 for 3 kg** or **$15 for 6 kg**?
tryIt.solution: $\$9/3 = \$3$/kg; $\$15/6 = \$2.50$/kg. The $15-for-6 kg is cheaper per kg.

steps.title: Which pack gives you more for your money?
steps.teach: Two packs of juice, different sizes. Tap **Reveal price per one** on each pack to find the cost for a single bottle. The smaller number wins.

steps.title: Why dividing by the quantity works
beats: From **Rates**: a rate compares two different amounts (like dollars and bottles). A **unit rate** goes one step further — the amount for exactly **one**.
**Dividing** the total price by the number of bottles shrinks the denominator to 1. That special case — amount for exactly one — is called the **unit rate**.
Small pack: 6 dollars ÷ 3 bottles $= 2$ dollars per bottle. Big pack: 10 dollars ÷ 4 bottles $= 2.50$ dollars per bottle.
Now both numbers are "per ONE bottle", so comparing is easy: 2 dollars is less than 2.50 dollars — the small pack is the better deal.

steps.title: Finding a unit rate
steps.intro: Divide the total by the number of units to get the amount for exactly one.

examples.prompt: A pack of 4 notebooks costs 12 dollars. What is the price **per notebook**?
steps: We want the price for **one** notebook, so we divide: $12 \div 4$.

examples.prompt: A car travels 300 km on 25 litres of fuel. Find the unit rate in **km per litre**.
steps: Per **one** litre means we divide distance by litres: $300 \div 25$.

steps.title: Your turn
steps.intro: Divide the total by the number of units. Tap the correct unit rate.

problems.prompt: 5 pens cost 20 dollars. What is the price **per pen**?
A cyclist rides 180 km in 6 hours. What is the speed in **km per hour**?
A tap fills 84 litres in 7 minutes. How many **litres per minute**?

steps.title: What is the unit rate?
steps.prompt: A bag of 8 apples costs 24 dollars. What is the price per apple?

steps.title: The tiny number on the shelf label
steps.body: Next time you shop, look at the small print in the corner of a price label — "2 dollars 50 per 100 g". That is the **unit rate**: the shop did the division so you can compare sizes fairly. Unit rates are on every shelf in every supermarket.

steps.title: Which bag is the better deal?
steps.teach: Reveal the price per kg for each bag. Remember: the **smaller** unit price wins — even if that bag costs more in total.

steps.title: A bigger total does NOT mean a worse deal
beats: The large bag (15 dollars) costs **more in total** than the small bag (9 dollars).
But: small bag $= 9 \div 3 = 3$ dollars per kg; large bag $= 15 \div 6 = 2.50$ dollars per kg.
The large bag is actually **cheaper per kg** — you get more rice for each dollar you spend.
Rule: always divide before you decide. Never judge a deal by the total price alone.

steps.title: Picking the better deal
steps.intro: Find the unit price of each option, then compare — lower unit price wins.

examples.prompt: Which is cheaper per bottle: 6 dollars for 3 bottles, or 10 dollars for 4 bottles?
steps: Compare: $2 < 2.50$, so Pack A is cheaper.
examples.answer: Pack A (6 dollars for 3 bottles) — 2 dollars per bottle

examples.prompt: Store A sells 5 eggs for 9 dollars. Store B sells 4 eggs for 7 dollars. Which store is cheaper per egg?
steps: Compare: $1.75 < 1.80$, so Store B is cheaper per egg.

steps.title: Your turn
steps.intro: Find each unit price, then pick the better deal.

problems.prompt: Which is cheaper per kg: 9 dollars for 3 kg, or 15 dollars for 6 kg?
options: 15 dollars for 6 kg — 2.50 dollars per kg
9 dollars for 3 kg — 3 dollars per kg
They cost the same per kg
problems.explanation: $9 \div 3 = 3$ dollars per kg; $15 \div 6 = 2.50$ dollars per kg. The large bag is cheaper per kg.

problems.prompt: Brand X: 2 litres of juice for 6 dollars. Brand Y: 3 litres for 12 dollars. Which is cheaper per litre?
options: They are the same price per litre
problems.explanation: Brand X: $6 \div 2 = 3$ dollars per litre. Brand Y: $12 \div 3 = 4$ dollars per litre. Brand X is cheaper.

steps.title: Which pack is the better deal?
steps.prompt: Pack A: 8 dollars for 4 biscuits. Pack B: 9 dollars for 3 biscuits. Which pack is the **better deal**?
steps.explanation: Pack A: $8 \div 4 = 2$ dollars each. Pack B: $9 \div 3 = 3$ dollars each. Pack A wins.

steps.title: What you learned
points: A **unit rate** is the amount for exactly **one** unit — divide the total by the count.
"Per" always means divide: price **per** bottle $=$ price $\div$ number of bottles.
To find the **better deal**, compute each unit price and pick the **smaller** one.
The bigger pack is **not always** cheaper — always divide before you decide.

steps.title: Time to practice
steps.body: You know how to find a unit rate and use it to compare deals. Lock it in with this mixed set — some ask for the unit rate, some ask which deal is better. Tap a wrong choice and just try again.

steps.title: Mixed practice
steps.intro: A little of everything you learned.

problems.prompt: 6 books cost 30 dollars. What is the price **per book**?
A runner covers 420 m in 60 seconds. What is the speed in **metres per second**?
Which is cheaper per pencil: 8 dollars for 4 pencils, or 9 dollars for 3 pencils?
problems.explanation: Pack A: $8 \div 4 = 2$ dollars. Pack B: $9 \div 3 = 3$ dollars. Pack A is cheaper.

problems.prompt: A hose fills 90 litres in 6 minutes. How many **litres per minute**?
Munkh earns 56 dollars in 8 hours. What is his **hourly rate**?
Store A: 12 eggs for 6 dollars. Store B: 8 eggs for 5 dollars. Which store is cheaper **per egg**?
problems.explanation: Store A: $6 \div 12 = 0.50$ dollars per egg. Store B: $5 \div 8 = 0.625$ dollars per egg. Store A is cheaper.

steps.title: You've finished "Unit Rate"
steps.body: Wages (dollars per hour), fuel economy (km per litre), internet speed (megabits per second), population density (people per km²) — almost every real-world measure is a unit rate. Every time you see the word **per**, you are reading one. You now have the tool to compare any two of them.

lessons.title: Ratio & rate word problems
lessons.concreteComparison: You're throwing a party. The punch recipe serves 6, but **15** friends are coming, and a poster says "2 cm on the map = 50 km in real life." Scaling recipes, reading maps, splitting costs — real life is one big word problem, and ratios are how you solve it.
lessons.objective: Set up and solve word problems by finding a unit rate or writing equal ratios, then scaling.
concept: Most ratio word problems are solved one of two ways: find the **unit rate** and multiply, or set up **two equal ratios** (a proportion) and scale.
Step 1: write the ratio with its words and units. Step 2: figure out the scale factor (or the unit rate). Step 3: apply it to the number you're asked about.
A quick sanity check: if you scaled *up*, your answer should be *bigger*; if you scaled *down*, smaller. If it's the wrong direction, you set the ratio up upside down.
lessons.keyIdea: Find the unit rate or write two equal ratios, then scale up or down. Always check your answer points the sensible direction.

facts.title: Proportion
facts.explanation: Two equal ratios. If three of the four numbers are known, you can find the fourth by scaling or cross-multiplying.

workedExamples.statement: If **4 concert tickets cost $52**, how much do **7 tickets** cost (same price each)?
workedExamples.note: Find the price of one ticket first, then multiply by 7.
workedExamples.solution: Unit price $= \frac{\$52}{4} = \$13$ per ticket. For 7 tickets: $13 \times 7 = \$91$. **Answer: $91.**

workedExamples.statement: A car travels **180 km in 3 hours**. At the same speed, how far does it go in **5 hours**?
workedExamples.note: Find the speed (unit rate), then multiply by 5 hours.
workedExamples.solution: Speed $= \frac{180}{3} = 60$ km/h. In 5 hours: $60 \times 5 = 300$ km. **Answer: 300 km.**

workedExamples.statement: Paint is mixed **red : blue = 3 : 5**. If you use **15 litres of blue**, how much red do you need?
workedExamples.note: Set up equal ratios: red/blue = 3/5 = ?/15. The blue went from 5 to 15 — a factor of 3.
workedExamples.solution: Blue scaled by $15 \div 5 = 3$, so red scales by 3 too: $3 \times 3 = 9$ litres. **Answer: 9 litres of red.**

commonMistakes.text: Setting the proportion upside down (putting blue over red on one side and red over blue on the other).
commonMistakes.correction: Keep the same order on both sides: red/blue = red/blue.

commonMistakes.text: Multiplying when you should divide (or vice-versa) because the unit rate wasn't found first.
commonMistakes.correction: Find the amount for ONE, then scale to the amount you need.

tryIt.statement: **6 pens cost $9.** How much do **10 pens** cost?
On a map, **2 cm = 5 km**. How many km is **8 cm**?
tryIt.solution: Per cm: $5/2 = 2.5$ km. For 8 cm: $2.5 \times 8 = 20$ km.

tryIt.statement: A ratio is **4 : 7**. If the 4-part equals **12**, what is the 7-part?

steps.title: Scale the paint mix
steps.teach: The base recipe is **3 red : 5 blue**. You set the blue amount — the tool finds the scale factor (new blue ÷ 5) and shows how much red you need. Same colour, bigger batch.

steps.title: Why the scale-factor move works
beats: Remember **Equivalent Ratios**: multiply both numbers by the same amount and the ratio stays the same — just a bigger batch.
The scale-factor move is exactly that: divide the known new amount by the base value to find the multiplier, then apply it to the other side.
Here, blue went $5 \to 15$, so the scale factor is $15 \div 5 = 3$. Red follows: $3 \times 3 = 9$.
**Sanity check**: you scaled *up*, so your answer should be *bigger* than the original. If it came out smaller, your ratio was upside down.

steps.title: Stretching a recipe
steps.intro: Find the scale factor (new ÷ base), then multiply the other side.

examples.prompt: A recipe uses **sugar : flour = 2 : 7**. You use **14 cups of flour**. How many cups of sugar?
steps: Flour went from 7 to 14. Scale factor: $14 \div 7 = 2$.
Sugar gets the same treatment: $2 \times 2 = 4$ cups of sugar.

examples.prompt: Concrete is mixed **gravel : sand = 4 : 3**. You pour **12 bags of gravel**. How many bags of sand?
steps: Gravel went from 4 to 12. Scale factor: $12 \div 4 = 3$.
Sand gets the same: $3 \times 3 = 9$ bags of sand.

steps.title: Your turn — stretch the recipe
steps.intro: Find the scale factor (new ÷ base), then multiply the other side.

problems.prompt: Juice is mixed **concentrate : water = 1 : 4**. You use **20 cups of water**. How many cups of concentrate?
problems.explanation: Water went from 4 to 20, so scale factor is $20 \div 4 = 5$. Concentrate: $1 \times 5 = 5$ cups.

problems.prompt: A paint colour uses **yellow : green = 3 : 2**. You have **6 cans of green**. How many cans of yellow?
problems.explanation: Green went from 2 to 6, so scale factor is $6 \div 2 = 3$. Yellow: $3 \times 3 = 9$ cans.

problems.prompt: A mortar mix is **cement : sand = 1 : 5**. You use **25 kg of sand**. How many kg of cement?
problems.explanation: Sand went from 5 to 25, so scale factor is $25 \div 5 = 5$. Cement: $1 \times 5 = 5$ kg.

steps.title: Stretch that recipe
steps.prompt: Orange squash is mixed **cordial : water = 2 : 9**. You use **18 cups of water**. How much cordial?
steps.explanation: Water went from 9 to 18, scale factor $= 18 \div 9 = 2$. Cordial: $2 \times 2 = 4$ cups.

steps.title: Recipes are ratios in disguise
steps.body: A traditional Mongolian buuz recipe keeps a fixed ratio of meat to dough — scale the batch up for a big family gathering and you use the same proportions every time. Cooks have been solving 'stretch the recipe' problems for thousands of years without ever writing an equation.

steps.title: Lemons per litre
steps.teach: The recipe is **4 lemons for every 1 litre** of lemonade. That rate (4 lemons per litre) is already a unit rate. Step the litres up and watch the lemons multiply: $4 \times \text{litres}$.

steps.title: Find the price for ONE, then multiply
beats: Sometimes you know the total for many, and need the total for a *different* number. The safe move: **divide to find ONE, then multiply to find N**.
4 concert tickets cost 52 dollars. How much do 7 tickets cost?
Step 1 — find the unit rate: $52 \div 4 = 13$ dollars per ticket.
Step 2 — scale to what you need: $13 \times 7 = 91$ dollars. This works for cost, distance, time, anything.

steps.title: Find ONE, then scale to N
steps.intro: Divide to get the unit rate, then multiply by whatever N you need.

examples.prompt: A car travels **180 km in 3 hours** at a steady speed. How far does it go in **5 hours**?
steps: Find the unit rate (speed): $180 \div 3 = 60$ km per hour.

examples.prompt: A tap fills **96 litres in 4 minutes** at a steady rate. How much does it fill in **7 minutes**?

steps.title: Your turn — find ONE, then multiply
steps.intro: Divide to find the amount for ONE, then multiply by the number you need.

problems.prompt: **3 notebooks cost 9 dollars.** How much do **5 notebooks** cost?
problems.explanation: One notebook: $9 \div 3 = 3$ dollars. Five notebooks: $3 \times 5 = 15$ dollars.

problems.prompt: A runner covers **30 km in 2 hours**. At the same pace, how far does she run in **5 hours**?
A machine makes **84 parts in 6 minutes**. How many parts does it make in **10 minutes**?
problems.explanation: Rate: $84 \div 6 = 14$ parts per minute. In 10 minutes: $14 \times 10 = 140$ parts.

steps.title: How much for 8?
steps.prompt: **6 pens cost 18 dollars.** How much do **8 pens** cost?
steps.explanation: One pen: $18 \div 6 = 3$ dollars. Eight pens: $3 \times 8 = 24$ dollars.

steps.title: What you learned
points: **Stretch a known ratio:** find the scale factor (new ÷ base), then multiply the other side by it.
**Find ONE, then multiply:** divide the total by the count to get the unit rate, then multiply by N.
**Sanity check:** scaling up gives a bigger answer; scaling down gives a smaller one. If it goes the wrong way, your ratio was upside down.
Both moves are the same idea underneath — equivalent ratios from the **Equivalent Ratios** chapter.

steps.title: Time to practice
steps.body: Both strategies in one mixed set. For each problem ask yourself: do I know the ratio and just need to scale? Or do I need to find the unit rate first? Then solve and check the direction.

steps.title: Mixed practice
steps.intro: A little of everything you learned.

problems.prompt: Orange paint is **red : yellow = 2 : 5**. You use **10 cups of yellow**. How much red?
**5 notebooks cost 45 dollars.** How much do **8 notebooks** cost?
A map uses **3 cm : 60 km**. A road is **5 cm** on the map. How long is it in real life?
problems.explanation: Per cm: $60 \div 3 = 20$ km. For 5 cm: $20 \times 5 = 100$ km.

problems.prompt: Concrete mix is **cement : gravel = 1 : 3**. You have **9 bags of gravel**. How many bags of cement?
A cyclist rides **45 km in 3 hours**. How far at the same pace in **7 hours**?
Purple dye is **red : blue = 3 : 4**. You use **12 cups of red**. How many cups of blue?

steps.title: You've finished "Ratio & Rate Word Problems"
steps.body: Find the scale factor or find the unit rate — those two moves unlock almost every ratio and rate word problem you'll meet. Maps, recipes, prices, speeds — they all run on the same math you just practised. You've finished the whole Ratios & Rates topic — well done!

practice.statement: A plane flies **210 km in 3 hours**. Find its speed.
Better deal: **$14 for 4 kg** or **$18 for 6 kg**?
practice.solution: $\$14/4 = \$3.50$/kg; $\$18/6 = \$3$/kg. The $18-for-6 kg is cheaper per kg.

practice.statement: **8 oranges cost $6.** How much do **12 oranges** cost?

testYourself.statement: A train covers **432 km in 6 hours**. Find its speed.
**$7.50 for 5 apples.** Find the price per apple.
A recipe uses **4 cups of flour to make 24 cookies**. How much flour for **60 cookies**?
testYourself.solution: Flour per cookie $= 4/24 = 1/6$ cup. For 60: $\frac{1}{6}\times 60 = 10$ cups.

testYourself.statement: A train goes **50 km in 40 minutes**. How far in **2 hours** (120 minutes), same speed?
testYourself.solution: Per minute: $50/40 = 1.25$ km. For 120 min: $1.25 \times 120 = 150$ km.
